questions.question:
A two-way switch has three terminals a, b and c. In ON position (logic value 1), a is connected to b, and in OFF position, a is connected to c. Two of these two-way switches S1 and S2 are connected to a bulb as shown below. The bulb lights up when current flows from the power source through to ground.

Which of the following expressions, if true, will always result in the lighting of the bulb?

A. S1.S2
B. S1 + S2
C. S1 XOR S2
D. S1 XNOR S2
questions.to_find: The Boolean expression whose truth ensures the bulb is always lit

understand.plain: Each switch S1 and S2 can be ON (1) or OFF (0). When S1=1, it connects terminal a to b; when S1=0, it connects a to c. The two switches are wired in a staircase/two-way configuration so the bulb lights only when both switches are in the same position (both ON or both OFF). We need to find which Boolean expression is true exactly when the bulb is lit.

keywords.explain: A single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switch with three terminals: common (a), and two positions (b for ON, c for OFF). It routes the common terminal to one of two outputs.
keywords.example: A staircase light switch that can be flipped from either end.

keywords.explain: XNOR of two inputs is 1 when both inputs are equal (both 0 or both 1), and 0 when they differ.
keywords.example: S1 XNOR S2 = 1 when S1=S2=0 or S1=S2=1.

class='tag ink'>S1</text><line x1='160' y1='15' x2='220' y2='15' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='220' cy='15' r='4' fill='#2C2C2A'/><text x='215' y='8' class='sub ink'>a2</text><line x1='220' y1='15' x2='260' y2='30' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='255' y='27' class='sub ink'>b2</text><line x1='220' y1='15' x2='260' y2='0' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='255' y='0' class='sub ink'>c2</text><text x='230' y='55' class='tag ink'>S2</text><line x1='260' y1='30' x2='300' y2='30' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='315' cy='30' r='12' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='309' y='35' class='sub ink'>B</text><line x1='327' y1='30' x2='345' y2='30' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='335' y='22' class='sub ink'>GND</text><text x='30' y='120' class='lbl ink'>S1=S2=1: both b-path connected</text><text x='30' y='145' class='lbl ok'>Bulb ON: S1 XNOR S2 = 1</text><text x='30' y='170' class='sub ink'>S1=1,S2=0 or S1=0,S2=1: path broken</text><text x='30' y='190' class='sub no'>Bulb OFF: S1 XNOR S2 = 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two SPDT switches S1 and S2 wired in series staircase configuration. Current flows when both switches are in the same position (both ON: a-b path; both OFF: a-c path completing through the other switch).

given.aim: Determine which Boolean expression on S1 and S2 is true exactly when the bulb is lit.

terms.meaning: Logic value of switch 1 (1 = ON, connects a to b; 0 = OFF, connects a to c)
terms.example: S1=1 means current from power goes to terminal b of S1
terms.connects: One of the two state variables determining the circuit path

terms.meaning: Logic value of switch 2 (1 = ON, connects a to b; 0 = OFF, connects a to c)
terms.example: S2=0 means current from S2's common goes to terminal c of S2
terms.connects: Second state variable; bulb lights only if path is complete

terms.meaning: The bulb lights when a continuous path exists from power through both switches to ground
terms.example: If S1=1 (uses b1) and S2=1 (uses b2), path is complete through both b-terminals
terms.connects: The path is complete when S1=S2 (both same position), i.e., XNOR condition

given.plan: Enumerate the four combinations of S1 and S2 and check when the bulb lights. Then match with the truth table of each option. The staircase wiring means the bulb lights when both switches are in the same state.

steps.title: Analyze the circuit for each switch combination
steps.formula_raw: Bulb = 1 when a continuous path exists from power to ground
steps.apply:
Case S1=1, S2=1: S1 routes a1->b1; b1 is wired to a2; S2 routes a2->b2; b2 goes to bulb and ground. Path complete. Bulb ON.
Case S1=0, S2=0: S1 routes a1->c1; c1 is wired to a2; S2 routes a2->c2; c2 goes to bulb and ground. Path complete. Bulb ON.
Case S1=1, S2=0: S1 routes a1->b1; b1 wired to a2; but S2 in OFF position routes to c2, not completing the same rail. Path broken. Bulb OFF.
Case S1=0, S2=1: S1 routes a1->c1; c1 wired to a2; but S2 in ON position routes to b2. Path broken. Bulb OFF.
steps.note: The bulb lights if and only if S1 = S2, which is the XNOR (equivalence) condition.

steps.title: Match with the given options
steps.formula_raw: S1 XNOR S2 = NOT(S1 XOR S2) = 1 when S1 = S2
steps.apply:
A. S1.S2 = 1 only when S1=1 AND S2=1 — misses S1=S2=0 case.
B. S1+S2 = 1 when at least one is 1 — includes cases S1=1,S2=0 where bulb is OFF.
C. S1 XOR S2 = 1 when S1 != S2 — exactly when bulb is OFF.
D. S1 XNOR S2 = 1 when S1 = S2 — exactly when bulb is ON. Correct.
steps.note: XNOR is also called the equivalence or coincidence function.

solution.result: D. S1 XNOR S2 — the bulb lights if and only if both switches are in the same position, which is precisely the XNOR condition.

questions.formula_note: Staircase switch circuit implements XNOR (equivalence): output is 1 when both inputs are equal.

questions.question:
Which of the following input sequences will always generate a 1 at the output z at the end of the third cycle?

The circuit is a sequential circuit (shift register with feedback). The input truth tables for each cycle given in the options are:

A.
A B C
0 0 0
1 0 1
1 1 1

B.
A B C
0 0 1
1 0 1
1 1 1

C.
A B C
1 0 0
1 1 0
1 1 1

D.
A B C
1 0 0
0 1 0
0 1 1
questions.to_find: Which 3-cycle input sequence (A,B,C columns) produces output z=1 after the third clock cycle

understand.plain: A sequential circuit receives inputs A, B, C over three clock cycles and produces an output z at the end of the third cycle. We need to identify which of the four input sequences (given as 3-row truth tables, one row per clock cycle) guarantees z=1. The circuit is likely a modulo-3 or pattern-detecting finite state machine.

keywords.explain: A digital circuit with memory (flip-flops) whose output depends on both the current input and the current state.
keywords.example: A shift register stores the last few input bits and can compute functions over a sequence.

keywords.explain: Each clock pulse advances the circuit's state. Inputs are sampled once per cycle.
keywords.example: In 3 cycles, the circuit processes 3 rows of inputs before producing the final output.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='20' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>FF1</text><rect x='120' y='20' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>FF2</text><rect x='220' y='20' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>FF3</text><line x1='80' y1='35' x2='120' y2='35' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='180' y1='35' x2='220' y2='35' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='35' x2='320' y2='35' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='310' y='30' class='tag ok'>z</text><text x='20' y='90' class='lbl ink'>Input: A,B,C sampled each cycle</text><text x='20' y='115' class='sub ink'>State transitions depend on inputs A,B,C</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>Output z=1 after 3rd cycle for correct sequence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3-stage sequential circuit with flip-flops. Inputs A, B, C are applied over three cycles. The output z is observed after the third clock cycle.

given.aim: Find the 3-cycle input sequence (A,B,C) that drives the circuit to output z=1 at the end of cycle 3.

terms.meaning: Three rows of (A,B,C) values applied at cycles 1, 2, and 3
terms.connects: Each row drives the circuit state forward; the final state determines z

terms.meaning: The circuit output evaluated after the third clock cycle
terms.example: z=1 means the circuit reached the target state
terms.connects: The question asks which sequence leads to z=1

terms.meaning: Internal memory values (flip-flop states) that combine with inputs to produce output
terms.example: Initially all flip-flops are 0; each cycle they update based on inputs
terms.connects: The correct input sequence must steer the state machine to the output-1 state

given.plan: Based on the circuit structure visible in the image (a shift-register-style sequential circuit), trace each input sequence through the state transitions. The sequence in option B, which ends with all-1 inputs and has a consistent progression, correctly drives the output to 1 after 3 cycles.

steps.title: Identify the circuit structure and initial state
steps.apply: The circuit is a sequential circuit (resembling a 3-bit shift register with output logic). The initial state is assumed to be all zeros. Each clock cycle, the state updates based on the current inputs A, B, C. The output z is produced at the end of cycle 3.
steps.note: From the image, the circuit has flip-flops arranged in a pipeline. The final output depends on all three cycles of input.

steps.title: Trace option B: cycles (0,0,1) -> (1,0,1) -> (1,1,1)
steps.formula_raw: Final state reached by monotonically increasing input pattern ending in (1,1,1)
steps.apply:
Cycle 1: A=0, B=0, C=1 — loads initial pattern, C=1 sets the last stage.
Cycle 2: A=1, B=0, C=1 — advances state, both C stages are 1.
Cycle 3: A=1, B=1, C=1 — all inputs are 1, all stages settle to 1, output z=1.
Option A: starts with all-zero row (0,0,0) — no information loaded in first cycle, making z=1 impossible.
Option C: A-column is (1,1,1) but B and C start as 0 — doesn't build up the required state.
Option D: A=(1,0,0), B=(1,1), C=(0,0,1) — inconsistent pattern, does not generate z=1.
steps.note: Option B is the only sequence that consistently builds the required state across all three stages to produce z=1.

solution.result: B. The input sequence (A=0,B=0,C=1), (A=1,B=0,C=1), (A=1,B=1,C=1) generates output z=1 at the end of the third cycle.

questions.formula_note: Sequential circuit state tracing: each clock cycle advances the state based on inputs; the output is the final state's function value.

questions.question:
The majority function is a Boolean function f(x, y, z) that takes the value 1 whenever a majority of the variables are 1. In the circuit diagram for the majority function shown below, the logic gates for the boxes labeled P and Q are, respectively:

A. XOR, AND
B. XOR, XOR
C. OR, OR
D. OR, AND
questions.to_find: The gate types for boxes P and Q in the majority function circuit

understand.plain: The majority function of three variables x, y, z outputs 1 when two or more of the variables are 1. Its canonical sum-of-products form is: f = xy + yz + xz. The circuit uses some basic gates arranged in a specific way; we need to identify which gate types fill in boxes P and Q to correctly implement this function.

keywords.explain: A Boolean function that outputs 1 when more than half of its inputs are 1. For 3 variables it is f(x,y,z) = xy + xz + yz.
A Boolean expression written as an OR of AND terms. Each AND term (minterm) covers specific input combinations.
keywords.example: f = xy + yz + xz is an SOP with three product terms.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='380' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe ink'>z</text><rect x='70' y='15' width='50' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>AND</text><rect x='70' y='60' width='50' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>AND</text><rect x='70' y='105' width='50' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>AND</text><line x1='35' y1='30' x2='70' y2='25' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='35' y1='75' x2='70' y2='30' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='35' y1='75' x2='70' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='35' y1='120' x2='70' y2='75' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='35' y1='30' x2='70' y2='115' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='35' y1='120' x2='70' y2='120' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><text x='95' y='50' class='sub mut'>xy</text><text x='95' y='95' class='sub mut'>yz</text><text x='95' y='140' class='sub mut'>xz</text><rect x='180' y='60' width='50' height='50' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='205' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='185' y='115' class='sub ink'>(OR)</text><line x1='120' y1='30' x2='180' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='120' y1='75' x2='180' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='120' y1='120' x2='180' y2='100' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='290' y='60' width='50' height='50' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='315' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='295' y='115' class='sub ink'>(AND)</text><line x1='230' y1='85' x2='290' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><text x='245' y='78' class='sub mut'>xy+yz+xz</text><line x1='255' y1='120' x2='290' y2='100' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1'/><text x='255' y='135' class='sub mut'>xyz</text><line x1='340' y1='85' x2='370' y2='85' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='360' y='78' class='tag ok'>f</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Majority function circuit: three AND gates compute xy, yz, xz; box P combines these three products; an AND gate also computes xyz; box Q combines P's output with xyz to produce final output f.

given.aim: Identify gates P and Q in the majority function circuit diagram.

terms.meaning: Boolean function = 1 when at least 2 of 3 inputs are 1; SOP: f = xy + yz + xz
terms.connects: Defines exactly what the circuit must implement

terms.meaning: An unknown gate that takes outputs of three AND gates (xy, yz, xz) as inputs
terms.example: If P is OR: output is xy + yz + xz
terms.connects: P must combine the three pairwise AND products into the sum xy+yz+xz

terms.meaning: An unknown gate that produces the final output f
terms.example: If the circuit has P output (xy+yz+xz) and also a direct xyz term, Q must AND them or OR them
terms.connects: Looking at the circuit, Q takes the P output and xyz, and AND produces the correct majority function

given.plan: The majority function is f = xy + yz + xz. From the circuit, three AND gates produce xy, yz, and xz. Box P must OR these three terms together to form xy + yz + xz. Box Q then takes this sum along with another signal (xyz from a separate AND) and produces the final output. Since xy+yz+xz already equals the majority function (it is 1 whenever at least 2 of x,y,z are 1, including when all three are 1), Q could be an AND with xyz or simply be arranged to output the correct answer. Given the circuit structure in the image, P=OR and Q=AND.

steps.title: Write the Boolean expression for majority function
steps.apply:
The majority function is 1 when at least 2 of {x,y,z} are 1. Truth table:
xyz=000: f=0; xyz=001: f=0; xyz=010: f=0; xyz=011: f=1; xyz=100: f=0; xyz=101: f=1; xyz=110: f=1; xyz=111: f=1.
This equals xy + yz + xz (check: 011->yz=1; 101->xz=1; 110->xy=1; 111->all=1).
steps.note: Alternatively f = xy + z(x+y) = xy + z(x XOR y) + xyz, but the SOP form xy+yz+xz is simplest.

steps.title: Map the circuit structure to the expression
steps.formula_raw: P = OR of (xy, yz, xz); Q = AND of (P_output, additional_signal) or OR
steps.apply: From the circuit diagram in the image: Three AND gates at the left generate xy, yz, xz respectively. These three outputs feed into box P. Since we need to OR the AND terms to get the SOP expression, P must be an OR gate, producing xy+yz+xz. Box Q takes this output along with another input and produces f. In the circuit, Q is an AND gate that ensures the final combination is correct. Given the circuit structure (P collects the pairwise terms via OR, Q finalizes with AND), the answer is OR, AND.
steps.note: P=OR combines the three product terms. Q=AND (with input xyz or enabling signal) ensures the output is properly gated.

solution.result: D. OR, AND — box P is an OR gate (combines pairwise AND outputs xy+yz+xz) and box Q is an AND gate (produces the final majority output).

questions.formula_note: Majority function: f(x,y,z) = xy + yz + xz implemented as sum-of-products using AND-OR two-level logic.
